subtopic_detail: Asymptomatic hypoglycaemia in infant of diabetic mother
stem: You are the paediatric registrar on the postnatal ward reviewing a 2-hour-old term male infant born by uncomplicated vaginal delivery at 39+2 weeks weighing 4.3 kg to a woman with insulin-treated type 2 diabetes. He had skin-to-skin contact and an attempt at breastfeeding at one hour of age but latched only briefly. The midwife performed a routine pre-feed blood glucose at 2 hours which is 1.8 mmol/L on a calibrated cot-side meter. The infant is alert, pink, breathing at 48/min with no recession, and has a normal cardiovascular and neurological examination.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142, RR 48, SpO2 97% in air, T 36.9 degrees C, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Vigorous, well-perfused, normal tone and reflexes, anterior fontanelle soft, no jitteriness

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the mother that this glucose is a normal physiological postnatal nadir in an infant of a diabetic mother and arrange a routine recheck only with the next scheduled three-hourly feed in approximately one hour
options.rationale: A BGL under 2.0 mmol/L in any at-risk infant always triggers active management and a 30-minute recheck after feeding, not passive observation until the next routine feed time. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Transfer the infant to the special care nursery immediately for insertion of a peripheral intravenous cannula and commencement of 10% dextrose at 60 mL/kg/day with hourly capillary glucose monitoring until two consecutive readings exceed 2.6 mmol/L
options.rationale: Intravenous 10% dextrose is reserved for symptomatic hypoglycaemia, BGL under 1.0 mmol/L, or persistent BGL under 2.6 mmol/L after feed plus buccal gel; an asymptomatic 1.8 mmol/L at first measurement is treated with feed first. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Administer 200 mg/kg of 40% dextrose gel to the buccal mucosa now without offering an oral feed and recheck the capillary blood glucose 30 minutes later before deciding on any further escalation of management or further dextrose
options.rationale: Buccal 40% dextrose gel is used in combination with a feed, not instead of one, and only after the first low BGL persists or as second-line for the next low reading per the Sugar Babies and Queensland pathway. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Encourage an immediate breastfeed or expressed-breast-milk top-up of 5 mL/kg and recheck the blood glucose 30 minutes after the feed before considering buccal dextrose gel or intravenous glucose escalation
options.rationale: ASID and Queensland Health newborn hypoglycaemia pathways treat an asymptomatic BGL of 1.0 to 2.5 mmol/L with an immediate feed of 5 mL/kg expressed colostrum or formula and a recheck 30 minutes after; persistent levels below 2.6 mmol/L then trigger 40% dextrose gel and senior review. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Send a critical sample for plasma glucose, insulin, cortisol, growth hormone, ketones and free fatty acids before any intervention because the most likely cause is congenital hyperinsulinism rather than transitional neonatal hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: A critical hypoglycaemia screen is appropriate when BGL stays below 2.6 mmol/L despite adequate feeds at over 48 hours of age, not at the first reading at 2 hours of life in an infant of a diabetic mother. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn hypoglycaemia)

explanation.summary: Infants of diabetic mothers are at high risk of transient hyperinsulinaemic hypoglycaemia from in-utero glucose exposure. Australian pathways treat asymptomatic BGL between 1.0 and 2.5 mmol/L with a 5 mL/kg feed of expressed breast milk or formula and a 30-minute recheck. Persistent low levels then trigger 40% dextrose gel (200 mg/kg = 0.5 mL/kg) plus a feed. Intravenous 10% dextrose is for symptomatic infants, BGL under 1.0, or failure of feed plus gel. The target threshold for treatment in the first 48 hours is 2.6 mmol/L.
key_points: IDM, LGA, SGA, late preterm are at-risk groups requiring screening
Treatment threshold first 48h: BGL under 2.6 mmol/L
First line: feed 5 mL/kg EBM or formula, recheck 30 min
Second line: 200 mg/kg of 40% dextrose gel buccally plus feed
IV 10% dextrose if symptomatic, under 1.0 mmol/L, or persistent failure
explanation.pearls: Buccal 40% dextrose gel dose is 0.5 mL/kg (200 mg/kg) given with a feed, not instead of one - this single dose has reduced NICU admission in the Sugar Babies trial.
